sanskrit: अयनेषु च सर्वेषु यथाभागमवस्थिताः। भिष्ममेवाभिरक्षन्तु भवन्तः सर्व एव हि॥
transliteration: ayaneṣu ca sarveṣu yathā-bhāgam avasthitāḥ। bhiṣmam evābhirakṣantu bhavantaḥ sarva eva hi॥
literal_meaning: All of you, stationed at every strategic point according to formation, must protect Bhishma alone—everyone indeed.
contextual_meaning: Duryodhana’s order reveals the anxious micromanagement of a leader who externalizes responsibility for safety onto others while denying his own moral bankruptcy; it mirrors how we often cling to a single ‘protector figure’ (status, parent, ideology) to avoid facing inner chaos. Psychologically, the verse exposes the defense mechanism of splitting—idealizing Bhishma as invulnerable while projecting one’s fragility onto the army’s formation, a collective denial of mortality and guilt.
psychological_state: A person frantically orchestrating others to guard a fragile idealized figure because acknowledging that figure’s fallibility would shatter their own identity.
trigger_scenario: A CEO ordering the entire team to protect the company’s ‘star’ manager from any criticism, secretly terrified that if the star fails, the whole firm—and their self-worth—will collapse.
anchor_text: Stop outsourcing your safety—face your fear instead of clinging to a fallible protector.
